---
title: "Automated Kellgren-Lawrence Grading of Knee Osteoarthritis Using Real-World Clinical Data: A Cross-Dataset Evaluation of Deep Learning Models Trained on Inferred Labels"
author: "Talita Mzendah"
bibliography: references.bib
csl: vancouver.csl
format:
  docx:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    fig-dpi: 300
    fig-width: 6.5
    fig-height: 4.5
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    embed-resources: true
    fig-width: 7
    fig-dpi: 150
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
---

# Cover Page {.unnumbered .unlisted}

::: {style="text-align: center; margin-top: 3em; margin-bottom: 2em;"}
![](cambridge_logo.png){width="40%" fig-align="center"}

<br><br>

### MSt Healthcare Data Science {.unnumbered .unlisted}
:::

#### Authentication of practice {.unnumbered .unlisted}

|   |   |
|---|---|
|I confirm that I have fully read and understood the assignment brief for this module.| **Y** |

#### Details {.unnumbered .unlisted}

|   |   |
|---|---|
|Name: Talita|Surname: Mzendah|
|Submission Date| 18 -- 06 -- 26 |
|Word Count: whole assignment including codes| |
|Word Count: main body excluding abstract, references and supplementary materials| |

#### Permission to share your assignment {.unnumbered .unlisted}

I do give permission to share my assignment with future MSt participants.

#### University statement of originality {.unnumbered .unlisted}

This assignment is the result of my own work and includes nothing which is the outcome of work done in collaboration except as declared in the Preface and specified in the text. It is not substantially the same as any that I have previously submitted for a degree or diploma or other qualification at the University of Cambridge or any other university or similar institution, or that is being concurrently submitted, except as declared in the Preface and specific in the text. I further state that no substantial part of my Portfolio has already been submitted, nor is being concurrently submitted for any such degree, diploma or other qualification at the University of Cambridge or any other university or similar institution except as declared in the Preface and specified in the text.

|   |   |
|---|---|
|I confirm the statement of originality as above| **Y** |

#### Questions for reflection {.unnumbered .unlisted}

Self-assessment is an important aspect of feedback literacy, which is, in turn, key to the development of expertise. As you proceed through the MSt Healthcare data science programme, we hope that you will make use of the following prompts to assess your own work on assignments. Specific assignment briefs will likely indicate which of these to address for which assessments, but, in general, we expect you to respond to one or two for each assignment on your course.

For each of the questions, do not spend too long answering -- keep it brief. For each question you answer, limit yourself to no more than three items. And please remember, this is optional and developmental: these cover sheets are designed to create space for self-assessment and feedback dialogue, rather than additional assignment workload.

1. Which aspects of this assignment are you most uncertain about and/or would most like to receive feedback on?

Whether the cross-dataset evaluation framework is methodologically appropriate for addressing the DL-inferred vs radiologist-graded label question, and whether the subgroup analysis is sufficiently powered given the sample sizes within demographic strata.

2. What elements are you left pondering after this assignment that you would like to discuss further?

How to best disentangle label noise from domain shift in the cross-dataset performance gap, and whether multimodal architectures incorporating clinical metadata would substantively change the fairness findings.

3. How have you incorporated feedback from peers and tutors into this assignment?

Peers recommended grounding the research question in a dataset limitation that is clinically meaningful rather than a purely technical comparison. This shaped the decision to focus on DL-inferred vs radiologist-graded labels as the primary research question, which has direct implications for how automated grading systems should be validated before clinical deployment.

4. How, and to what extent, have you been able to incorporate feedback on previous course work into this assignment?

Following feedback on previous coursework, I prioritised reproducibility and transparency — all code, configuration files, and split CSVs are available in the accompanying repository, and the filter cascade is documented step-by-step to enable exact replication.

5. Using the wording in the rubric, how would you describe the quality of the different aspects of your work?

I would describe this as an excellent attempt addressing all rubric elements. The methodological depth — particularly the filter cascade, DICOM conversion pipeline, and patient-level split — reflects careful attention to data integrity. The subgroup analysis framework directly addresses fairness requirements for clinical AI systems.

#### Declaration of the use of generative AI {.unnumbered .unlisted}

|   |   |
|---|---|
| Which permitted use of generative AI are you acknowledging? | Code generation, informed feedback |
| Which generative AI tool did you use (name and version)? | Claude Code Sonnet 4.6 |
| What did you use the tool for? | Code generation, informed feedback, reference curation |
| How have you used or changed the generative AI's output? | |

{{< pagebreak >}}

## Abstract

**Background:** Knee osteoarthritis (OA) is a leading cause of musculoskeletal disability, affecting an estimated 300 million people globally. Radiographic severity is classified using the Kellgren-Lawrence (KL) scale, with automated grading using convolutional neural networks (CNNs) showing considerable promise. However, most published systems are trained on small, demographically homogeneous research cohorts with radiologist-assigned labels, raising questions about real-world generalisability. A critical but underexplored issue is whether models trained on deep learning-inferred KL grade labels — as found in large real-world clinical repositories — can generalise to radiologist-graded ground truth.

**Methods:** Three CNN architectures (ResNet50, DenseNet121, EfficientNet-B0) were trained on a stratified random sample of 10,000 knee radiographs (2,000 per KL grade 0–4) drawn from the MRKR dataset [@price2024emorykneeradiographmrkr] — a multi-site clinical repository of 503,261 images from Emory University Health System with DL-inferred KL labels. Images were resized to 512×512 pixels following a one-time DICOM-to-PNG conversion pipeline. A patient-level 70/15/15 stratified split was applied to prevent data leakage. External validation was performed on the OAI/Kaggle dataset [@chen2018knee; @shashwat2021] (radiologist-graded ground truth) without retraining. Subgroup analysis stratified performance by sex, race, ethnicity, age group, weightbearing status, and laterality.

**Results:** [XX — to be completed after training]

**Conclusions:** [XX — to be completed after training]

## 1. Introduction

Osteoarthritis (OA) is the most prevalent musculoskeletal (MSK) condition globally, affecting an estimated 300 million people worldwide and representing a leading cause of chronic pain and functional disability [@litjens2017]. Radiographic grading of knee OA severity is central to clinical decision-making, guiding referral pathways, monitoring disease progression, and determining eligibility for intervention. The Kellgren-Lawrence (KL) scale remains the international standard for radiographic OA grading, classifying severity from KL0 (normal) to KL4 (severe) based on the presence and extent of osteophytes, joint space narrowing, subchondral sclerosis, and bony deformity [@kellgren1957].

Among the five grades, KL1 — doubtful OA — carries disproportionate clinical significance. Missing KL1 may delay risk-factor modification, monitoring, or clinical follow-up in symptomatic or high-risk patients. Without timely identification, the opportunity for risk-modifying strategies — weight management, physiotherapy, and activity modification — is lost, and the patient may only re-present once structural damage has progressed to an irreversible stage. Early detection is therefore not a diagnostic nicety but a clinically consequential decision with direct implications for long-term patient outcomes.

The application of deep learning to automated KL grading has grown substantially, with CNNs demonstrating promising performance on large radiographic datasets [@tiulpin2019]. However, existing systems face two underappreciated limitations that constrain their clinical relevance. First, most published studies use radiologist-graded labels from standardised research protocols — particularly the Osteoarthritis Initiative (OAI) — which may not reflect the variation in real-world clinical acquisition. Second, very few studies evaluate performance across clinically meaningful demographic subgroups, leaving performance disparities across age, sex, race, and acquisition type unquantified [@obermeyer2019].

The MRKR dataset [@price2024emorykneeradiographmrkr] provides an opportunity to address both limitations. Comprising 503,261 knee radiographs from Emory University Health System across multiple clinical sites and a decade of acquisition, with 40% African American representation, MRKR enables training on real-world clinical data at scale. However, MRKR KL labels are assigned by a validated deep learning inference model rather than direct radiologist annotation — raising a primary research question: **can a CNN trained on DL-inferred KL grade labels generalise to radiologist-graded ground truth?** A secondary question concerns fairness: **does model performance vary systematically across clinically meaningful patient subgroups?**

This study addresses these questions by training and comparing three CNN architectures (ResNet50, DenseNet121, EfficientNet-B0) on MRKR, with external validation on the OAI/Kaggle dataset and comprehensive subgroup analysis across demographic and acquisition variables.

## 2. Methods

### 2.1 Primary Training Dataset — MRKR

The MRKR (Musculoskeletal Radiology Knowledge Repository) dataset was used as the primary training dataset [@price2024emorykneeradiographmrkr]. MRKR comprises 503,261 knee radiographs from 77,215 patients across multiple clinical sites at Emory University Health System, acquired over a ten-year period under real-world clinical conditions. It is publicly accessible via the AWS Open Data Registry under a data use agreement requiring institutional registration, licensed under CC-BY-SA.

KL grades in MRKR are assigned by a validated deep learning inference model (L_KLG_inference, R_KLG_inference) rather than direct radiologist annotation. This is a deliberate and methodologically significant choice: the MRKR labelling approach has been validated in peer-reviewed literature, and whether models trained on such inferred labels can generalise to human radiologist performance is the primary research question of this study.

### 2.2 External Validation Dataset — OAI/Kaggle

External validation was performed using the Osteoarthritis Initiative (OAI) knee radiograph dataset distributed via Kaggle [@chen2018knee; @shashwat2021], comprising approximately 9,786 knee images with KL grades assigned by trained radiologists following the OARSI grading protocol. These human consensus labels constitute a qualitatively different form of supervision from MRKR's inferred labels. Cross-dataset evaluation directly tests the central research question. No retraining was performed on this dataset; it was used for inference only.

### 2.3 Filter Cascade and Working Set Derivation

A principled filter cascade was applied to the full MRKR metadata (503,261 rows) to derive a clinically appropriate working set. Filters were applied sequentially: (1) frontal views only (view_position == F), reducing the dataset from 503,261 to 205,121 images — lateral, sunrise, and oblique projections were excluded as KL grading requires anteroposterior frontal views; (2) known laterality (laterality != −1), removing 202 images; (3) arthroplasty exclusion (arthroplasty == 0), removing 50,401 post-operative knees where KL grading is inapplicable; (4) bilateral image expansion — images acquired bilaterally were expanded into two rows, one per knee, each labelled independently from the corresponding inference column, increasing the row count to 235,789; (5) valid label range (0–4), removing 73,581 rows with NaN or out-of-range labels, yielding a final working set of 162,208 labelled knee-image pairs. From this working set, a stratified random sample of 2,000 images per KL grade (n=10,000 total) was drawn, including both weightbearing and non-weightbearing acquisitions to preserve real-world variation.

### 2.4 Demographics Merge

Patient-level demographic information (sex, race, ethnicity) from MRKR_demographics.csv was merged onto the image-level working set via the anonymised patient identifier (empi_anon). Age at examination was binned into decade groups (<40, 40–49, 50–59, 60–69, 70+). These variables were retained as metadata and were not used as model inputs; they were used exclusively for post-hoc stratified performance evaluation.

### 2.5 DICOM to PNG Conversion

MRKR images are stored in DICOM format with JPEG Lossless compression (Transfer Syntax 1.2.840.10008.1.2.4.70). A one-time conversion pipeline was implemented using pydicom and pylibjpeg to decompress and normalise each image. Pixel values were linearly normalised to the range [0, 255] as unsigned 8-bit integers. Photometric inversion was corrected using the DICOM PhotometricInterpretation tag: images stored as MONOCHROME1 (bright = air, dark = bone) were inverted to standard radiographic convention (MONOCHROME2). All images were saved as three-channel RGB PNG files for compatibility with ImageNet-pretrained backbone architectures. This conversion was performed once prior to training.

### 2.6 Train/Validation/Test Split

Data were partitioned into training (70%), validation (15%), and test (15%) sets using a patient-level stratified split. Because bilateral expansion produces two image rows per bilateral examination, a naive row-level split would risk placing both knees from the same patient in different partitions, constituting data leakage. Stratification was therefore performed at the patient level (empi_anon) using the majority KL grade per patient. The resulting splits comprised approximately 7,000 training, 1,500 validation, and 1,500 test images, each maintaining the balanced grade distribution.

### 2.7 Preprocessing and Augmentation

All images were resized to 512×512 pixels. This resolution was selected following exploratory data analysis of the MRKR dataset, which revealed a median image resolution of 3,028×2,539 pixels: downsampling to 224×224 pixels would result in loss of fine-grained joint space detail critical for distinguishing KL grades 0, 1, and 2, and was therefore clinically inappropriate for this task.

Training augmentation was intentionally conservative: random horizontal flip (p=0.5) — valid as MRKR includes both left and right knees — and small random rotation (±5°) were applied for positional invariance. Mild colour jitter (brightness and contrast ±15%) accounted for scanner and exposure variation across clinical sites. Aggressive spatial augmentation was avoided to prevent distortion of joint space geometry. Validation and test images received no augmentation. All images were normalised using ImageNet statistics (mean=[0.485, 0.456, 0.406]; std=[0.229, 0.224, 0.225]).

### 2.8 Model Architectures

Three CNN architectures were evaluated, selected for architectural diversity and established performance in KL grading literature.

**ResNet50** [@he2016], a 50-layer residual network with approximately 23.5 million parameters, was selected as the benchmark architecture. Tiulpin et al. [-@tiulpin2019] trained a ResNet50 ensemble on OAI and demonstrated generalisation to the independent MOST dataset, establishing ResNet50 as the reference for KL grading benchmarking. **DenseNet121** [@huang2017densenet] was selected based on competitive performance in comparative KL grading studies [@gour2023]. The dense connectivity pattern — each layer receiving feature maps from all preceding layers — encourages feature reuse and compact representations particularly beneficial for the subtle texture-level differences between early KL grades. DenseNet121's suitability for radiographic tasks is further supported by its strong performance in chest X-ray pathology detection. **EfficientNet-B0** [@tan2019efficientnet] was selected following findings by Dipto and Goni [-@dipto2024] that EfficientNet ensembles outperformed other architectures for KL grading. Its compound scaling strategy achieves competitive accuracy at approximately 4 million parameters, a practically relevant advantage for deployment in resource-constrained clinical settings.

All architectures were initialised with ImageNet-pretrained weights. The final classification layer of each backbone was replaced with a linear layer mapping to five output classes (KL grades 0–4).

### 2.9 Training Configuration

All models were trained using the AdamW optimiser with weight decay 1×10⁻⁴. A ReduceLROnPlateau scheduler reduced the learning rate by 50% when validation balanced accuracy plateaued for three consecutive epochs. Early stopping was applied with patience of 7 epochs, saving the checkpoint with the highest validation balanced accuracy. Maximum training was capped at 30 epochs with a batch size of 32. Class imbalance was addressed at the data level through stratified sampling of 2,000 images per grade, producing a balanced training set; no loss weighting or oversampling was required. A single random seed (42) was used, applied to Python, NumPy, PyTorch, and CUDA random number generators, with deterministic CUDA operations enabled. All training was performed on the Cambridge Service for Data Driven Discovery (CSD3).

### 2.10 Evaluation Metrics

Model performance was evaluated exclusively on the held-out test set. **Balanced accuracy** (mean per-class recall) was the primary metric, treating all five KL grades equally and remaining insensitive to class frequency — appropriate given the clinical importance of detecting minority grades including KL1. Additional metrics included macro F1, mean absolute error (MAE) of grade prediction, macro AUC, per-grade precision, recall, F1, and confusion matrices. **KL Grade 1 recall** was designated the primary clinical outcome. All metrics were computed identically on the internal MRKR test set and the OAI/Kaggle external validation set to enable direct comparison.

### 2.11 External Validation

All three MRKR-trained models were evaluated without retraining on the OAI/Kaggle test set. The performance gap between MRKR internal test set and OAI/Kaggle was interpreted as a combined measure of label noise (DL-inferred vs human consensus) and domain shift (multi-site real-world vs standardised research acquisition). Disentangling these two sources requires future work with matched acquisition protocols.

### 2.12 Subgroup Analysis

Test set performance was stratified across the following subgroups to assess fairness and generalisability:

| Variable | Categories | Clinical Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Weightbearing status | WB / non-WB | Acquisition affects joint space width |
| Sex | Male / Female | OA prevalence differs by sex |
| Race | Caucasian / African American / Other | Algorithmic fairness |
| Ethnicity | Hispanic / non-Hispanic | Supplementary fairness axis |
| Age group | <40, 40–49, 50–59, 60–69, 70+ | OA increases with age |
| Laterality | Left / Right | Laterality bias check |

: Table 1. Subgroup analysis variables, categories, and clinical rationale.

No ethics approval was required as both datasets are publicly available and contain no personally identifiable information.

## 3. Results

*All numeric results marked [XX] are placeholders to be completed following training completion.*

### 3.1 Dataset Characteristics

Following the filter cascade, the final working set comprised 162,208 labelled knee-image pairs drawn from the full MRKR repository of 503,261 images. The stratified sample of 10,000 images (2,000 per grade) was drawn from this working set. The filter cascade stages and case counts are summarised in Table 2.

| Filter step | Images remaining | Removed |
|---|---|---|
| Full MRKR repository | 503,261 | — |
| Frontal views only | 205,121 | 298,140 |
| Known laterality | 204,919 | 202 |
| Arthroplasty excluded | 154,518 | 50,401 |
| Bilateral expansion | 235,789 | +81,271 |
| Valid label (0–4) | 162,208 | 73,581 |
| **Stratified sample** | **10,000** | — |

: Table 2. MRKR filter cascade — images retained at each step.

Demographics of the 10,000-image working set are summarised in Table 3.

| Variable | Category | n (%) |
|---|---|---|
| Sex | Female | [XX] ([XX]%) |
| | Male | [XX] ([XX]%) |
| Race | Caucasian | [XX] ([XX]%) |
| | African American | [XX] ([XX]%) |
| | Other / Unknown | [XX] ([XX]%) |
| Ethnicity | Non-Hispanic | [XX] ([XX]%) |
| | Hispanic | [XX] ([XX]%) |
| Age group | <40 | [XX] ([XX]%) |
| | 40–49 | [XX] ([XX]%) |
| | 50–59 | [XX] ([XX]%) |
| | 60–69 | [XX] ([XX]%) |
| | 70+ | [XX] ([XX]%) |
| Weightbearing | Weightbearing | [XX] ([XX]%) |
| | Non-weightbearing | [XX] ([XX]%) |

: Table 3. Demographic and acquisition characteristics of the 10,000-image working set.

### 3.2 Model Comparison — MRKR Internal Test Set

| Metric | ResNet50 | DenseNet121 | EfficientNet-B0 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Balanced accuracy | [XX] | [XX] | [XX] |
| Macro F1 | [XX] | [XX] | [XX] |
| MAE | [XX] | [XX] | [XX] |
| Macro AUC | [XX] | [XX] | [XX] |
| KL1 recall | [XX] | [XX] | [XX] |
| KL1 F1 | [XX] | [XX] | [XX] |

: Table 4. Primary metrics on the MRKR held-out test set (n≈1,500).

### 3.3 Per-Grade Performance

| KL Grade | ResNet50 P/R/F1 | DenseNet121 P/R/F1 | EfficientNet-B0 P/R/F1 |
|---|---|---|---|
| KL0 (Normal) | [XX]/[XX]/[XX] | [XX]/[XX]/[XX] | [XX]/[XX]/[XX] |
| KL1 (Doubtful) | [XX]/[XX]/[XX] | [XX]/[XX]/[XX] | [XX]/[XX]/[XX] |
| KL2 (Mild) | [XX]/[XX]/[XX] | [XX]/[XX]/[XX] | [XX]/[XX]/[XX] |
| KL3 (Moderate) | [XX]/[XX]/[XX] | [XX]/[XX]/[XX] | [XX]/[XX]/[XX] |
| KL4 (Severe) | [XX]/[XX]/[XX] | [XX]/[XX]/[XX] | [XX]/[XX]/[XX] |

: Table 5. Per-grade precision / recall / F1 on the MRKR held-out test set.

### 3.4 External Validation — OAI/Kaggle

| Metric | MRKR test set | OAI/Kaggle | Gap |
|---|---|---|---|
| Balanced accuracy | [XX] | [XX] | [XX] |
| Macro F1 | [XX] | [XX] | [XX] |
| KL1 recall | [XX] | [XX] | [XX] |

: Table 6. Best-performing MRKR-trained model: internal vs external validation performance (OAI/Kaggle, radiologist-graded). Gap = MRKR test set minus OAI/Kaggle.

### 3.5 Subgroup Analysis

| Subgroup | n | Balanced accuracy | KL1 recall |
|---|---|---|---|
| Female | [XX] | [XX] | [XX] |
| Male | [XX] | [XX] | [XX] |
| Caucasian | [XX] | [XX] | [XX] |
| African American | [XX] | [XX] | [XX] |
| Weightbearing | [XX] | [XX] | [XX] |
| Non-weightbearing | [XX] | [XX] | [XX] |
| Age <50 | [XX] | [XX] | [XX] |
| Age 50–69 | [XX] | [XX] | [XX] |
| Age 70+ | [XX] | [XX] | [XX] |

: Table 7. Subgroup performance for the best-performing architecture on the MRKR test set. Subgroups with substantially lower balanced accuracy or KL1 recall are flagged for clinical attention.

## 4. Discussion

### 4.1 DL-Inferred Labels and the Generalisation Question

The primary research question of this study — whether a CNN trained on DL-inferred KL grade labels can generalise to radiologist-graded ground truth — has direct implications for how automated grading systems should be validated before clinical deployment. DL-inferred labels offer a practical pathway to large-scale supervised training without the cost of radiologist annotation, but introduce a systematic source of uncertainty: the labelling model's own failure modes are inherited by any classifier trained on its outputs [@price2024emorykneeradiographmrkr]. The performance gap observed between the MRKR internal test set and OAI/Kaggle external validation reflects the combined effect of this label noise and domain shift between multi-site real-world acquisition and the standardised OAI protocol. Disentangling these two sources is a priority for future work and requires a matched evaluation design in which the same patients are imaged under both conditions — a study design not currently achievable with available open datasets.

### 4.2 Why KL1 Remains Hard

KL Grade 1 is expected to remain the most challenging grade to detect regardless of label type or architecture, for reasons that no modelling choice alone can resolve. The radiographic changes at KL1 are subtle — small osteophytes at joint margins and equivocal joint space narrowing — and inter-rater variability at KL1 is well documented even among experienced radiologists [@tiulpin2019]. When labels are inferred by a DL model rather than assigned by a radiologist, this variability is compounded by the labelling model's own uncertainty at the KL0/KL1 boundary, introducing label noise concentrated precisely at the grade most challenging to detect. The KL scale itself does not account for age-related normal variation in bone morphology, meaning elderly patients may be systematically over-graded at KL1 and younger patients under-graded, a demographic confound invisible to image-only models without clinical metadata.

### 4.3 Fairness and Demographic Generalisability

MRKR's demographic composition — with approximately 40% African American representation — provides an unusually diverse training set relative to most published KL grading studies, which have predominantly used OAI, a predominantly Caucasian research cohort [@obermeyer2019]. This diversity creates an opportunity to evaluate whether automated KL grading performance is equitable across racial and ethnic subgroups — a question that cannot be answered with OAI-trained models due to insufficient demographic representation. Performance variation across subgroups, if observed, has direct clinical implications: a system that performs substantially worse for African American patients or for non-weightbearing acquisitions would codify existing healthcare inequalities if deployed without subgroup-level validation. The MRKR subgroup analysis addresses this gap directly, and any subgroup with substantially lower balanced accuracy or KL1 recall should be flagged as a deployment risk.

### 4.4 Architectural Comparison

The three architectures selected represent a spectrum of established approaches in the KL grading literature. ResNet50 serves as the benchmark following Tiulpin et al. [-@tiulpin2019]; DenseNet121 has demonstrated competitive performance in comparative KL grading studies [@gour2023], and its dense connectivity pattern offers potential advantages for subtle grade-level texture discrimination; EfficientNet-B0 provides parameter efficiency relevant for resource-constrained deployment [@dipto2024; @tan2019efficientnet]. Differences across architectures on the same dataset and training procedure reflect genuine differences in inductive bias rather than differences in data or optimisation, providing a controlled comparison.

### 4.5 Clinical Implications

These findings have direct implications for the design of automated KL grading systems for clinical deployment. A model trained on DL-inferred labels may achieve reasonable internal test performance while showing substantially degraded performance on radiologist-graded external data — the magnitude of this gap determines whether the model is safe to use as a clinical screening tool. Even a high-performing model should be deployed as a decision support tool rather than a replacement for clinical judgement, and its known failure modes — particularly KL1 under-detection — should be communicated to reporting clinicians to support appropriate scepticism. The integration of clinical metadata (age, sex, weight-bearing status, body mass index) alongside image features represents a necessary direction for future development and is likely a prerequisite for automated grading to reach the sensitivity required for safe clinical deployment at the KL1 threshold.

## 5. Ethical Considerations

Automated KL grading systems carry several ethical considerations that must be addressed before clinical deployment. First, such systems should function as decision support tools rather than replacements for clinical judgement: no current automated system is sufficiently sensitive to operate independently at the early OA threshold without clinician oversight. Second, the absence of demographic metadata in OAI/Kaggle — and the DL-inferred rather than radiologist-assigned labels in MRKR — means that potential performance disparities remain partially unquantified, and deploying a system with unknown subgroup performance risks codifying existing healthcare inequalities [@obermeyer2019]. Third, transparency and explainability are essential for clinical trust: clinicians relying on automated grading outputs should be informed of the model's known failure modes, particularly its tendency to misclassify KL1, to support appropriate scepticism when interpreting borderline cases. Both datasets used are publicly available under open licences and contain no personally identifiable information, satisfying data governance requirements for research use. Code and reproduction instructions are available at: <https://github.com/tmzendah/HCDS_Mod05>.

## 6. Conclusion

This study addresses a clinically relevant and underexplored question: whether CNN architectures trained on DL-inferred KL grade labels from a large, demographically diverse real-world clinical repository (MRKR) can generalise to radiologist-graded ground truth (OAI/Kaggle). Three architectures — ResNet50, DenseNet121, and EfficientNet-B0 — were trained on a balanced sample of 10,000 MRKR images and evaluated internally and on the external OAI/Kaggle dataset. [XX — results and conclusions to be completed after training.] KL Grade 1 remains the most clinically consequential grade to detect and is expected to represent the primary performance challenge across all architectures. Subgroup analysis across sex, race, ethnicity, age, and weightbearing status directly addresses algorithmic fairness — a prerequisite for responsible deployment of automated grading systems in diverse clinical populations. Future work should prioritise multimodal architectures incorporating clinical metadata alongside image features, and longitudinal validation on prospectively acquired datasets with both standardised and real-world acquisition conditions.

## References
